Pilate understood the difference between armed forces and Jesus’ followers. However, instead of being moved by Pilate's declaration of his innocence, and considering whether they were bringing guilt for innocent blood upon themselves, the Jews became even more angry. The Lord brings his plans to a glorious end, even through those who follow their own desires. Thus all parties joined together, so as to prove the innocence of Jesus, who was the atoning sacrifice for our sins.

Herod had heard many things about Jesus in Galilee and longed to see him out of curiosity. The poorest beggar who asked for a miracle for relief was never denied; but this proud prince, who asked for a miracle only to satisfy his curiosity, was refused. He could have seen Christ and his wondrous works in Galilee, and did not, therefore it is justly said that now he would see them, and would not be allowed. Herod sent Jesus again to Pilate: the friendships of wicked men are often formed by agreement in wickedness. They agree on little except their enmity toward God and contempt for Christ.

The fear of man leads many into a trap, causing them to do unjust things against their consciences rather than face trouble. Pilate declares Jesus innocent and intends to release him; yet, to appease the people, he would punish him as an evil-doer. If no fault is found in him, why chastise him? Pilate eventually yielded; he lacked the courage to go against such strong opposition. He handed Jesus over to their will, to be crucified.

Here we have the blessed Jesus, the Lamb of God, led as a lamb to the slaughter, to sacrifice. Though many reproached and reviled him, some felt pity for him. But the death of Christ was his victory and triumph over his enemies; it was our deliverance, the purchase of eternal life for us. Therefore, do not weep for him, but let us weep for our own sins, and the sins of our children, which caused his death; and weep for fear of the miseries we will bring upon ourselves if we reject his love and grace. If God handed him over to such sufferings because he was made a sacrifice for sin, what will he do with sinners themselves, who make themselves a dry tree, a corrupt and wicked generation, and are good for nothing! The bitter sufferings of our Lord Jesus should inspire awe of the justice of God. The best saints, compared with Christ, are like dry trees; if he suffered, why may they not expect to suffer? And what then will be the damnation of sinners! Even the sufferings of Christ preach terror to stubborn wrongdoers.

As soon as Jesus was fastened to the cross, he prayed for those who crucified him. The great thing he died to purchase and secure for us is forgiveness of sin. This is what he prays for. Jesus was crucified between two thieves; in them were shown the different effects the cross of Christ would have on people when preaching the gospel. One thief remained hardened to the end. No troubles alone will change a wicked heart. The other was softened at the last: he was rescued from destruction and became a testament to God’s mercy. This does not encourage anyone to postpone repentance until their deathbeds or to hope that they will find mercy then. It is certain that true repentance is never too late; but it is equally certain that late repentance is seldom true. No one can be sure they will have time to repent at death, but everyone can be sure they cannot have the advantages this penitent thief had. We will see a rare case if we observe the uncommon effects of God’s grace on this man. He rebuked the other for cursing Christ. He admitted that he deserved what was happening to him. He believed Jesus suffered unjustly. Observe his faith in this prayer. Christ was at the depth of disgrace, suffering as a deceiver, and not delivered by his Father. He made this declaration before the wonders were displayed which honored Christ's sufferings and startled the centurion. He believed in a life to come and desired to be happy in that life, unlike the other thief, who sought only to be saved from the cross. Observe his humility in this prayer. His request is simply, Lord, remember me; completely entrusting it to Jesus as he sees fit. Thus he was humbled in true repentance, and brought forth all the fruits of repentance that his circumstances allowed. Christ on the cross is gracious like Christ on the throne. Though he was in the greatest struggle and agony, he felt pity for a poor penitent. By this act of grace we are to understand that Jesus Christ died to open the kingdom of heaven to all repentant, obedient believers. It is a single instance in Scripture; it should teach us not to despair of anyone, and that no one should despair of themselves; but lest it be abused, it is contrasted with the terrible state of the other thief, who died hardened in unbelief, though a crucified Savior was so near him. Generally, men die as they live.

Here we see the death of Christ magnified by the wonders that accompanied it, and his death explained by the words he spoke as he breathed out his soul. He was willing to offer himself. Let us seek to glorify God through true repentance and conversion; by protesting against those who crucify the Savior; by a sober, righteous, and godly life; and by using our talents in service of Him who died for us and rose again.

Many, though they do not outwardly profess it, will be as ready to do real service, like Joseph of Arimathea, when there is occasion, than others who make a greater show. Christ was buried hastily because the Sabbath approached. Weeping must not hinder preparation. Though they were saddened by the death of their Lord, they needed to prepare to keep holy the Sabbath. When the Sabbath approaches, there must be preparation. Our worldly affairs must be arranged so that they do not interfere with our Sabbath work; and our spiritual affections so stirred up that they carry us on in it. In whatever business we engage, or however our hearts may be affected, let us never fail to get ready for, and keep holy, the day of sacred rest, which is the Lord’s day.
